data.title: 3 Things To Consider In Kevin Warsh's Nomination For Federal Reserve Chair
data.description:
President Trump’s nomination of Kevin Warsh as the Chairman of the Federal Reserve led to an unusually sharp sell-off in metals. Viewed as preserving the central bank’s independence, gold (XAUUSD:CUR), silver (XAGUSD:CUR), and copper prices fell. Gold spot prices fell by 9.6%, while silver dropped by around 28%. Platinum prices fell, while the U.S. dollar (DXY) snapped back.

What does the nomination mean for U.S. stock markets, interest rates, and the bond outlook?

1/ U.S. Stock Markets

First and foremost, investors might dismiss the violent sell-off in metals on Friday. Silver, gold, copper, and platinum prices increased too fast. By mid-week, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell, and Microsoft (MSFT) led the decline in AI-related names. Markets needed any excuse to set the temporary, profit-taking event.

In the chart below, the sell-off in SaaS (software as a service, or subscription-based sellers) had nothing to do with the Fed Chair nomination. Adobe (ADBE), AppLovin (APP), Salesforce (CRM), Autodesk (ADSK), and SAP (SAP) fell in the last week.

Excessive valuations in software names, except for Adobe, are one of the reasons for the weekly decline. Look at the Seeking Alpha Valuation grades, shown above.

Look instead at the U.S. dollar (UUP) from here. The currency basket snapped back on January 29, after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that the U.S. would not intervene in Japan. While it supported a “strong dollar policy,” he would let Japan’s currency valuation increase. This stance, along with the FOMC Chair nomination, strengthened the U.S. dollar.

The renewed strength in the dollar might alleviate the market’s fear of a currency collapse. It will put aside concerns that hurt it in the first place: tariffs and other policies.

Stock markets are not likely to move in either direction in a big way. The S&P 500 (SPY) rose modestly by around 1.3%. This is underperforming non-U.S. indices. As an extreme example, iShares MSCI Peru (EPU) and iShares MSCI South Korea (EWY) are up by 19.8% and 28.87%, respectively.

2/ Interest Rates

Markets will put aside fears that the nominee would weaken the FOMC’s independence. Earlier this week, the Fed held rates steady at 350 – 375 bps, as market participants largely expected. After the Fed issued its statement, Fed Chair Powell commented that democratic countries operated under an independent central bank system. This is central to democracy.

As the next Chair, investors should expect that Warsh will maintain the practice of data-driven monetary policy decisions. Inflation, through the monthly consumer price index reports, and employment, through non-farm payroll data, are two of many data points that the Fed will use. Assuming that tariffs remain in place when Warsh is Chair, the Fed will use tools to monitor their impact on the economy.

3/ Bond Market Outlook

After the nomination, neither the 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) nor the 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) moved by much. The calm reaction strengthens the attractiveness of continuing to hold U.S.-based debt.

To manage risks, markets will instead monitor Japan’s bond market. The 10-year and 40-year bond yields traded at levels not seen since 1999. This encourages investors to unwind their carry trade. That might help the U.S. bond market if investors buy that debt to park their assets. However, U.S. stocks trading at unsustainable valuations are at a higher risk of pulling back.

Your Takeaway

The stock market’s strong performance under Jerome Powell reflected his leadership in the central bank, achieving maximum employment at stable prices. Although he was “late” in raising interest rates in 2022, he acted quickly to recognize inflationary pressures. This partly led to the soft landing.

The more the new Chair keeps the Fed out of politics, addressing its mandate of maximum employment at stable prices, the more stability Warsh will bring to stock and bond markets.

data.title: Keyence: Eyes On Record-Breaking Quarter And Dividend Upside (Upgrade)
data.description:
I've revised Keyence Corporation's (KYCCF) (6861.T) rating from "Hold" to "Buy."

Its Q3FY2026 (YE March) earnings/share surpassed expectations. There's also room to further penetrate foreign markets.

I see the group paying out a more sizeable share of its profit in the future, considering external and internal developments.

The manner in which KYCCF deploys its monies was the subject of my previous Sept. 19, 2025, update.

I'm Encouraged By Best-Ever 3Q Performance

The firm's latest financials were revealed on Thursday, Jan 29.

The bottom-line rose +9% year-on-year to ¥459/share in Oct-Dec '25. This was equivalent to a 10% outperformance versus S&P Capital IQ consensus.

It registered a more rapid pace of expansion for the recent three-month timeframe.

KYCCF's 3QFY26 turnover increase was +11.4% on YoY terms, nearly doubling the +5.9% seen in 2QFY26. During the same period, its EBIT growth went from 1.6% to 8.4%.

The group's topline, operating income, and EPS represented all-time peaks for the December-end quarter in its history.

In my opinion, the Japanese company's efforts to venture outside its borders have paid off.

Non-domestic sales were +17% higher in 3QFY2026 and accounted for seven-tenths of the group-level figure.

Institutional investor VanEck drew attention to China's "accelerating adoption of factory automation" in its 2025 commentary. This helps me to understand why KYCCF's Asian revenues were up +21.1% last quarter.

Its American region witnessed a similarly impressive 17.5% rise for Q3. That's aligned with SA analyst "Luca Socci's" view that the United States is moving to a "state-backed industrial policy, driving massive investments in manufacturing."

I had called KYCCF "a seller of factory automation products" in my mid-Sep '25 write-up.

Geographical Segment Projections

As per the chart presented above, Asia's "Total Addressable Market/TAM" is nine times as big as its home market. But the group's Japanese sales are still more substantial for now, implying a long runway ahead.

Separately, KYCCF's "Gross Profit Margin/GPM" widened by +30bps (basis points) sequentially to 83.1% for 3QFY26. This is also 0.5ppts (percentage points) better than the 1st quarter's 82.6%.

I wrote in the earlier Nov 1, 2024 article that the company "rolled out a number of new products" catering to "unmet client needs" which should "command a price premium." These initiatives are reflected in its improving GPMs.

My take is that KYCCF will continue to do well going forward. My 4QFY2026 top-line and EBIT growth projections are +11% and +12%, respectively. I've considered the major levers like international penetration and product portfolio optimization.

Anticipating More Generous Capital Returns

My prior Sep 19 piece highlighted that "I'm unsatisfied with its stockholder returns." I mentioned specifically about its "modest (trailing) dividend yield of 0.6%" and low "FY25 dividend-to-earnings ratio" of "21%."

There are reasons for me to believe that positive adjustments are coming.

Fund manager "Halvio Capital's" early-2026 letter indicated that Japan's "FSA (Financial Service Agency)" is "working on a revision of the governance code to have each company assess whether they are using their cash resources effectively."

KYCCF had cash and investment of almost ¥3 trillion ($20 billion) on its books as of Dec 31, 2025. This makes up one-fifth of its market cap. You would expect the firm to be under scrutiny from the regulatory authorities, if the situation remains unchanged.

Also, I need to give credit to the enterprise for making some progress on this front.

KYCCF's full-year FY26 dividend/share guide was lifted from ¥350 to ¥550 when it announced Q2 results in Oct '25. The implied payout is an improved 30%. It should certainly share more of its profits with stockholders. The average for Japanese equities is two-fifths.

Late last year, analysts from UBS Group's (UBS) met with the group's management. The bank's Dec 3, 2025 report (not publicly available) cited comments from "Director and IR Officer" suggesting that "we want the equity market to recognize Keyence is changing." This was in response to discussions about the company's capital policy. It sends a clear signal to me that further cash distribution hikes are highly probable.

My forecasts point to the stock's proportion of earnings distributed and yield rising to the mid-30% range and over 1%, respectively for FY2027.

You Should Be Mindful Of These Variant Views

Weaker-than-expected economic conditions in Americas/Asia could lead to slower sales for KYCCF.

It might persist with a "lazy" balance sheet. There may be a resulting governance-related discount assigned to the shares.

My Conclusion

I'm becoming bullish on this name.

KYCCF is now valued by the market at 19.7 times EV/EBITDA (source: S&P Capital IQ). Its five-year mean multiple was 27.1x.

The stock's re-rating potential is +37% (27.1/19.7) benchmarked against history.

It's at the early stages of "going global." There is also upside associated with dividends. These are the catalysts warranting a richer EBITDA ratio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kevin Warsh: Big Changes (Other Than Rates) May Be Coming To The Fed
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, President Donald Trump named Kevin Warsh to be the next Chairman of the Federal Reserve. Warsh will succeed Jerome Powell, whose term expires in May. Warsh must still go through the confirmation process to get the job, a situation that is far from a done deal considering a Republican Senator has already shared his reservations this morning. While many investors are focused on the Federal Reserve’s fed funds rate and President Trump’s insistence that it be lowered, there are some other monetary policy beliefs of Kevin Warsh that investors should be aware of should he be confirmed as Fed chair.

Kevin Warsh’s Resume

Over the last fifteen years, Kevin Warsh has served as a Distinguished Visiting Fellow of Economics at the Hoover Institution at Stanford University. His focus has been on monetary policy and financial stability. There are many notable economists in the Hoover Institution’s history, with Milton Friedman and Thomas Sowell being among them. In addition to this role, Warsh has served as an advisor and partner to Stanley Druckenmiller’s family office since 2011.

The most applicable experience in Kevin Warsh’s resume was his previous service on the Federal Reserve’s Board of Governors from 2006 to 2011. While his five-year stint is important, I believe the history that unfolded during his term is equally important. During his last stint, Warsh witnessed the financial crisis, which became the world’s most challenging economic crisis since the Great Depression and the introduction of Quantitative Easing, where the Central Bank bought U.S. Treasuries to keep interest rates low and supply the financial system with an increased money supply.

Recent Comments Regarding Interest Rates

It was clear from the beginning of this process that President Trump wanted someone who supported lower interest rates. Interestingly, while Warsh has called for lower interest rates, he has not specified exactly how much the Fed should cut, when the Fed should cut, or what (if any) the neutral interest rate should be.

With Warsh’s guidance being unclear, it’s hard to project how much, if any, his influence will change the trajectory of the Federal Reserve. The Chairman of the Federal Reserve does carry influence but ultimately represents only one vote out of a twelve-person body. As it currently stands, nine or ten FOMC participants tend to vote for the monetary policy path per meeting. The change of one voting individual will not influence the committee further unless President Trump is able to make more appointments to FOMC.

The Area to Watch- The Fed’s Balance Sheet

While everyone has been monitoring President Trump’s fight with the Federal Reserve and discussing the importance of the Central Bank’s independence, Kevin Warsh has made some comments to suggest that he may try to change the way the Federal Reserve operates and change its multi-trillion-dollar balance sheet.

When it comes to operations, Warsh may be steering the Fed away from the dot plot, otherwise known as the committee’s economic projections, which are released quarterly. Warsh has been a constant critic of the dot plot, stating that it makes the committee too restrictive in handling changes and forces them to be “prisoners of their own words.”

Where I believe that investors need to pay the most attention is with respect to Warsh’s comments regarding the balance sheet. Warsh believes that the balance sheet should be smaller, with the Central Bank carrying less US Treasuries. The least intrusive way for the Fed to do this would be to allow Treasuries on its balance sheet to mature without reinvestment, which would then require one of the many actors who purchase US Treasuries to step up to the plate and buy more.

Skepticism of Quantitative Tightening

While quantitative tightening (QT) would be welcomed by some in the financial world, it comes with its own set of risks. QT involves a reduction in the money supply, which is deflationary, as the Central Bank inserts bond securities in the market in exchange for cash. This could have a damaging effect on pricing, which Warsh argues then necessitates the use of rate cuts to stabilize prices.

The greater unknown is liquidity, which is the amount of free-floating cash circulating in the economy. I’ve written about my concerns surrounding economic liquidity in the past and that there needs to be at least an equal amount of money available to meet the demands of the assets being bought and sold on the open market. Liquidity continues to remain tight, and I’m not certain how QT can be accomplished under these circumstances.

Conclusion

The appointment of Kevin Warsh still needs Congressional confirmation, and that is likely to be anything other than smooth. His appointment, however, opens the door for sweeping changes to the Federal Reserve beyond monetary policy. While investors eye interest rates, the balance sheet and the way the Federal Reserve communicates with investors could see drastic changes soon.

data.title: Meta Platforms Exemplifies How Fanciful AI Pursuits Damage Shareholder Value
data.description:
Too often, in the current investing environment, investors are making a fundamental category error in evaluating the current market leaders, the so-called “Magnificent 7.” The structure of the error goes something like this. Investors look at Mag 7 companies and say, “Hey, they’re highly profitable and they do AI, thus we have proof of concept that AI can be a profitable venture, unlike the speculative mania of the dot-com bubble. Thus, the present environment is nothing like the dot-com bubble. Everything is fine.”

The problem is that the Mag 7 companies (and really I’m talking about Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG), (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META) here) are not profitable because they do AI; instead, they are profitable despite doing AI. These companies have core, non-AI business lines that account for their profitability, and, largely speaking, the AI ventures function as money-losing side bets for them. In this article, I’m going to use Facebook and Instagram parent Meta Platforms as a case study to show how AI-related ventures are detracting from the shareholder value created by Mag 7 companies rather than contributing to it.

Earnings Recap

META released their earnings for the fourth quarter and full year 2025 after the market closed on Wednesday, January 28. Here is the full press release. I would like to highlight a couple of things:

  1. While revenues for the full year were up 22% y/y, costs and expenses were up 24%. Even more concerning, while revenues for the quarter were up 24% y/y, costs and expenses were up 40%.
  2. In line with this, operating margins for the full year decreased y/y from 42% in 2024 to 41% in 2025. And, again, more concerningly, operating margins for the quarter decreased from 48% in the year-ago period to 41% in the latest quarter.
    • These first two points are in line with the idea that the company is seeing a decrease in operational leverage as its AI capex spending over the last several years starts to come through in the income statement through depreciation charges. Put another way, the numbers here are clearly showing the degradation to the business quality that all the AI-related capex is creating.
  3. Headcount increased by 6% y/y. This indicates that, as we enter the fourth year of “the AI revolution,” at a company where one should expect to see such things first, AI is, in fact, not leading to a measurable decrease in headcount. This would seem to run counter to the narrative that AI is going to lead to reduced headcounts.
  4. Capex for 2025 was $72.22 billion—35.9% of 2025 revenue, 62.4% of 2025 cash flows from operating activities.
  5. Proposed capex for 2026 is $115-$135 billion. Given that the company forecasted at the beginning of 2025 that they would spend between $60-65 billion in capex for 2025, assuming that the company spends at the high end of the proposed 2026 range (i.e., they spend $135 billion in 2026) seems like a conservative estimate to me. Thus, this implies the company will have 2026 capex at 54.8% of estimated 2026 revenues and 93.3% of my estimate (assuming a 25% growth from 2025 numbers) of total cash flow from operations.
    • In short, the company plans to dramatically accelerate their capex spend in 2026 in pursuit of their AI ambitions from an already head-spinning 2025 AI-related capex spend number.

Should Shareholders Want All This AI-Related Capex?

Given that we’re already seeing the deterioration of the business quality from all this AI-related capex (increasing costs, decreasing margins, stalling free cash flow numbers (see below)), shareholders need to ask themselves whether they’re really ok with META potentially doubling their capex numbers on a y/y basis in 2026 from an already incredibly high base.

If you are going to lean so heavily into AI capex, it seems you’re going to do it for one of two reasons. Either you are trying to make AI tools with specialized areas of operations—e.g., an LLM to enhance ad targeting, a coding LLM, a financial modeling LLM, a protein-folding LLM, etc.; or you are pursuing something like artificial general intelligence (AGI).

Now, some people think we should read the stock market’s reaction to META’s earnings (META is up 10.24% at the time of writing) as the market reading META as showcasing more immediate ROI from AI investments. This would interpret META’s AI ambitions as being centered around specialized tools with specific domain applicability—e.g., enhancing ad targeting and ROI therefrom. However, it is not at all clearly obvious how one should interpret META’s AI ambitions and their massive capex spending commitments. Even if this was why they were spending all this money on AI, it’s not clear that this is even the best strategy. It seems to me that Apple’s strategy of letting others do the grunt work of developing AI models, taking on all the capex and risk in pursuit of a technology that is already largely becoming commoditized, and then just paying to use their services for your various applications is a better way to approach the situation.

No, I think META’s goal that rationalizes all this AI capex is the pursuit of the holy grail of artificial general intelligence (AGI). However, the pursuit of AGI through scaling LLMs is not at all an obvious path to success here, even though it’s the only obvious path out there that necessitates all this capex (after all, covering the earth in data centers full of GPUs costs a lot of money, not to mention the cost of potentially putting them in space). Notable luminaries in the field have argued as much, including Ilya Sutskever, Demis Hassabis, Gary Marcus, and, most notably in the current context, former META Chief AI Scientist Yann LeCun.

Yet, Mark Zuckerberg is throwing hundreds of billions of dollars of your capital, dear META shareholder, into this speculative AI gold rush. In doing this, he is degrading the economics of a world-class, capital-light core business. He is taking a pristine capital-light business model and ratcheting up the capital intensity such that now the company is converting far less revenues to free cash flows than it otherwise would have (see below discussion regarding free cash flows for further discussion of this unnecessary incremental cash burn—i.e., we're not seeing negative free cash flow numbers here, just far less free cash flow conversion than otherwise would be and far less conversion than there has been historically in percentage terms). META’s current AGI ambitions have all the potential to make the failed Metaverse look like a child’s failed lemonade stand by comparison.

Let’s take a closer look at the damage to shareholder value these AGI ambitions are already resulting in and can be expected to result in going forward for META shareholders.

Quantifying the Damage to Shareholder Value

As already alluded to, the damage to shareholder value resulting from AI capex is clearly present in META’s just-released earnings report—costs rising faster than revenues (again, think accelerating depreciation charges) and operating margins deteriorating.

However, beyond this, the story of the damage to shareholder value becomes more obvious when we look at per-share free cash flow numbers (my preferred metric of profitability):

As we would expect, the chart has an “up and to the right” character to it of the sort we would expect from the world-class sort of company META has been historically. However, since the release of ChatGPT in late 2022, the progression of FCF/share numbers has been stymied, with FCF/share actually decreasing y/y from 2024 to 2025. And this is all despite revenues having grown at a CAGR of ~22% over the period from 2023 to 2025.

This becomes more obvious when we look at a chart of quarterly per-share free cash flow numbers since 2023:

This chart has none of the “up and to the right” character one would expect of a company growing revenues at a rate of roughly 20-25% and supposedly being the sort of cash cow many investors picture when they think of META. Instead, one is left with the impression of meandering numbers that are largely stalling out, consistent with the same period from the yearly chart above.

But this is what should be expected when management is taking the cash a high-quality core business spits off and throwing it into the highly speculative gold rush that is the pursuit of AGI. Rather than investing in the high-quality core ads business that seemingly has a long runway and excellent prospects for profitability, investors in META today are betting on a sacrifice of the quality of that core business at the altar of Zuckerberg’s pursuit of AGI. If it pays off, the returns will, of course, be big. If it does not, I think investors should expect returns far below the expectations implied by the current valuation multiples of the stock.

Putting it All Together

In short, I am dumbfounded at how investors are currently looking at the same report I am looking at and are deciding to reward the company’s performance by bidding up the stock by over 10%. The fact that META investors don’t balk at Zuckerberg’s plans to potentially double AI-related capex over the next year is utterly confounding. In the past, META investors have suffered significantly due to Zuckerberg’s Metaverse pet project. And now, as he proposes to accelerate his pursuit of AGI by potentially nearly doubling AI-related capex from an already high base (and, in so doing, potentially make the Metaverse look like mere child’s play), I don’t understand how META investors have not learned their lesson. If you go long on META’s stock here, you are not buying a high-quality ads business. Instead, you are buying a high-quality ads business that Zuckerberg is deciding to sacrifice at the altar of speculative bets pursuing AGI, bets that there are good reasons to think are highly likely to fail and thus be a total waste of shareholder capital.

Risk to Thesis

The main risk to the thesis here, of course, is that META is ultimately successful in its pursuit of AGI. If anyone ever actually achieves AGI, they will, of course, unlock immense value. However, one has to assess such things on an expected-value basis. If the cost was only a few billion here and there, then this pursuit might make sense. But, when we talk about spending potentially the entirety of META’s annual cash flow from operations in the pursuit of AGI along a trajectory that isn’t anywhere close to obviously going to promise near-term results, this strikes me as a foolhardy bet, to say the least.

Investor Takeaway

While investors’ reaction to Microsoft’s earnings suggests some potential element of rationality reasserting itself, with investors’ reaction to META’s earnings, we see that the AI bubble still lives on. The question remains how much more cash investors will allow to go up in smoke with no real results before they start to demand accountability for the allocation of their capital. For me, the capital allocation decisions being made at META are highly suspect, and Zuckerberg does not, in my opinion, seem to be acting like a good steward of shareholder capital. Thus, my recommendation is ‘sell.’

data.title: Rolls-Royce: Still Higher To Go
data.description:
My last article on the British aviation company Rolls-Royce (RYCEY) in early December 2025 was titled "Rolls-Royce: Buy The Dip." This title followed an 11% dip in price in less than two months, which was entirely contrary to the upside indicated by both the fundamentals and the market valuations.

So, it's no surprise that the stock is up by over 21% since. But with that much rise in less than two months, the question now is whether the Buy case for RYCEY persists for right now.

The Market Multiples

Let's first consider the market multiples. With no financial updates for the company since I last checked, my projections for its net profits for 2025 remain unchanged at $3.64 billion.

This brings the forward P/E to 38.3x, which is expectedly higher than the level of 32.2x in December. But it's still lower than the stock's 5-year average of 42.4x. This indicates that there's still over 10% upside to RYCEY, and that's only on the basis of the expected results for 2025. Next, for 2026, my net income estimate is $3.99 billion, which results in a forward P/E of 34.9x and an upside of 20% for this year.

The Fundamentals (and Other Stock Metrics)

The positive market multiples are backed by both fundamentals and other stock metrics. These are as below.

#1. Civil Aerospace's Growth Continues

The company's civil aerospace business has started 2026 on the right note with the order of 62 engines from Delta Air Lines (DAL). For context, in H1 2025, the latest numbers available, the company supplied 122 large engines. Assuming that the company supplied double that number for the full year 2025, it has already achieved a quarter of that in the first month of 2026.

The progress of Rolls-Royce's civil aerospace segment is particularly important since it's the biggest for the company, contributing to over half the revenues and almost 70% of the underlying operating profit in H1 2025.

#2. Nuclear Focus Expands

A few months ago, I had made a case for higher market valuations for RYCEY based on the potential of its fledgling nuclear energy business. Not only is there increasing policy support for the energy source, but Rolls-Royce's SMR development has shown strong potential too.

The company continued to gather momentum on this front since I last checked as well. It has now partnered with engineering firms Assystem, AtkinsRéalis, and Frazer-Nash to realize an agreement with the UK's Ministry of Defence that entails providing nuclear power plants required for its use of Rolls-Royce's submarines.

While it can take time for nuclear energy to make a dent in the company's financials, the continued developments are a sign that the segment is on its way to becoming potentially significant to the company.

#3. Share Buybacks Can Continue

After a GBP 1 billion ($1.38 billion) share buyback initiated with its full-year 2024 results on February 27, 2025, which ran until November of last year, Rolls-Royce initiated another such program to run from January 2 to February 26, 2026, for $200 million.

This could have had a positive impact on the share price at the margin as well, and in conjunction with last year's program, it can reflect positively on the EPS for 2025 as well. With the company's continued good financial health, a continuation of buybacks in 2026 can't be ruled out either.

#4. Dividend Yield Can Rise

Further, the dividend yield can also well rise. For 2025, the company paid out 30% of its underlying net profit as dividends. Based on the current market capitalization, this results in a trailing twelve months [TTM] dividend yield of 0.68%. If the company continues to pay the same 30% as dividends in 2026, the forward dividend yield rises to 0.78%.

This isn't the biggest dividend yield around by any stretch, for sure, but over time, the numbers can add up. Consider this. For an investor who bought RYCEY at the price of $7.26 at the start of 2025, a dividend per share in 2026 of $0.13 (based on 30% of the expected net income of $3.64 billion in 2025 and 8.4 billion shares), the yield rises to 1.78%.

What Could Hold RYCEY Back?

Everything is looking very well for Rolls-Royce, so let's temper that with a consideration of any factors that can cause pullbacks in the stock, even if temporarily. Here are two such.

  • Analysts' Estimates Indicate Fair Valuation

My financial estimates are more bullish than those of analysts on Seeking Alpha, which put the forward P/E at 45.1x, indicating the the stock is at least fairly, if not overvalued right now. For 2026, the P/E is at 38.9x, which indicates upside, but limited to sub-10%.

This means that the upside reflected by my estimates might not come to pass. At the same time, it's worth noting that the company's earnings have surprised on the upside consecutively for the past three years. And not by a small margin. The EPS has exceeded estimates by anywhere between 10%-55%.

If earnings in 2025 surprise by 28%, which is the mean surprise rate between 2022 and 2024, the actual EPS would rise to $0.49, and the P/E would be at 35x. This is lower than even the 38.3x indicated by my estimates.

  • The Question of Defense

Another risk, if it can be called that, is that defense-related stocks can slide if peace between Russia and Ukraine is successful. This is a limited risk, though. I had covered this point in greater detail in my previous article on the stock (see sub-segment "Defense segment can be affected by a truce" in the first link of this article), so I won't go over it again.

In brief, though, defense is neither the biggest nor the most profitable line of business for the company. Moreover, defense spending has been increased by countries around the world in any case, as a deterrent.

The Stockholm International Peace Research Institute finds that in 2024, global military spending rose by 9.4% YoY, the biggest rise since 1988. It further adds that "Many countries have also committed to raising military spending, which will lead to further global increases in the coming years." This means that Rolls-Royce's defence segment can stay in demand even as peace ensues.

What's Next?

In the almost two years that I've covered Rolls-Royce here on Seeking Alpha, I've had a consistent Buy rating on the stock. And indeed, it has performed, with over a 200% increase since. For reference, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up by a little over 33% over this time.

Now, RYCEY is trading at levels not seen in over a decade. It might have come a long way, but I believe it still has further to go, as is evident from both the indications from the market multiples as well as the fundamentals. I'm maintaining the Buy rating yet again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Microsoft: The Earnings Drawdown Is A Gift (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) just released its Q2 FY2026 earnings, and there is a lot to take away, as highlighted by the biggest single-day drop in stock price for the tech giant since the COVID pandemic. I rated it a Buy earlier in the month and want to see how this changes the thesis.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first dive into the numbers, with revenue surging to $81.3B, up 16.8% Y/Y and beating the analyst forecast of $80.2B. On the bottom line side, Q2 Non-GAAP EPS stood at $4.14, a beat of 22 cents and again a massive surge of 24% Y/Y. Net Income GAAP was also significantly boosted by a $7.6B gain from investments in OpenAI (OPENAI).

Diving deeper, Intelligent Cloud has been the big winner of the cycle with an astonishing 29% Y/Y growth rate as Azure revenue grew by a massive 39%, fueled by AI demand. CEO Satya Nadella confirmed that demand continues to exceed the available capacity, which is a great tailwind to have. But apparently, even as Microsoft is building data centers as fast as possible, the company can’t meet every customer’s request for GPU power. And this will compound as the company’s commercial bookings grew by a massive 230% due mainly to the massive multi-year cloud commitments from AI labs like Anthropic (ANTHRO) and OpenAI. The backlog has reached an astonishing $625B, which is more than 7x Microsoft’s total revenue for the entire quarter, guaranteeing a long-term revenue stream as these AI projects ramp up.

On another AI front, Microsoft 365 Copilot reached 15 million paid seats, showing a strong transition from testing AI to paying for it. This is in a rapid scaling phase, which is a 160% Y/Y increase in seat additions, showing that there are company-wide rollouts. The E5 license, which is the most expensive tier, remains a primary growth driver, as customers are upgrading not just for security but also because it is the easiest foundation for layering on AI tools. GitHub Copilot also now has 4.7MM paid subscriptions and remains the most successful pro AI tool in the company’s arsenal, fundamentally changing how software is written within the company’s ecosystem.

For the Personal Computing side, it was the only segment that saw a revenue decline, at -3%, but it is important to dive deeper into the strategic shift. Xbox consoles are seeing sales crater by more than 32%, and thus, Microsoft has been pivoting towards being a platform-agnostic publisher as it has boosted its game publishing business with the deal for Activision-Blizzard. With time, the company would rely less and less on Xbox sales. Windows OEM revenue is also benefiting from the Windows 10 end-of-life boost, which pushed revenue to rise by 5%, with the hardware refresh cycle as businesses scramble to upgrade to Windows 11 before Windows 10 support officially ended a couple of months ago.

Despite all of these positives, the stock dropped like a stone following three primary anxieties that spooked the street. First, the OpenAI concentration revelation as MSFT disclosed that 45% of its massive backlog of $625B is directly tied to OpenAI. Now this actually means that a massive chunk of Microsoft’s future growth is actually just one customer buying back the computing power that MSFT founded with its own multi-billion dollar investment. This raises alarm bells, as the growth seems less organic and more dependent on a single startup’s survival.

The second argument would be that CapEx is accelerating while the guidance has been slowing. The company spent a staggering $37.5B on CapEx this quarter, which is a 66% increase, mainly on data centers and AI chips. Azure is posting immense growth, yes, but the company guided 37-38% from an increase of 39%. The fear is that Microsoft is overbuilding infrastructure for demand that might not materialize fast enough to justify the bill.

And on a third point, the growth is limited by hardware capacity and not customer demand, and as MSFT cannot build data centers fast enough to sell more services, investors hate a growth ceiling. If it can’t deploy the chips it's buying fast enough, competitors with better supply chains or more efficient infrastructure could steal market share.

Though in my opinion, the three pillars are concerning, they resemble more of a temper tantrum from the street than a failure of fundamentals. As I said, the capacity constraint is a high-class problem to have. This implies a massive coiled spring of revenue, and as the new Fairwater super factories come online in early 2026, the pent-up demand will convert into recognized revenue. Microsoft is intentionally rationing GPUs, prioritizing its own high-margin products like Copilot over lower-margin third-party Azure rentals. This is a move for profitability and not a sign of weakness, in my opinion.

Looking at the CapEx figure, much of this will be spent on Maia 200 and Cobalt 200 chips. Microsoft is building its own hardware and moving toward a future where it doesn’t have to pay the Nvidia (NVDA) tax, thus significantly boosting its cloud margins in 2027 and beyond.

Valuation

Thanks to the drawdown, Microsoft now has one of the most compelling valuations among the large hyperscalers. It currently has a P/E of 28.19, lower than Apple (AAPL) at 32.65 or Amazon (AMZN) at 34.14, despite having the better growth prospects and the strongest revenue growth over the past 3 years. Though it is currently generating extreme levels of Free Cash Flow, with $77.4B generated during the last twelve months, the company’s heightened stock prices currently make it have a FCF yield of 2.2%. Though I would say that right now, you are getting the world’s most dominant enterprise software and cloud company at a 10% discount while its core cloud engine is still growing at 39%, a rate almost unheard of for a company of this size.

I believe that the company’s current transition phase is masking the true growth potential. While the market is overreacting to the massive spending, the underlying growth engines are actually accelerating in ways that are rare for a company of this scale. The RPO of $625B is currently a massive savings account for contracted revenue that Microsoft simply hasn’t unlocked yet, as they are still building the data centers to host the workloads.

I’m also highly bullish on the $30/month Copilot tier. Right now, there are 15 million paid seats, but that is only 3.3% of the 450 million users of Microsoft 365. If the company can move just 10% of its users, this would add $16B in high-margin annual revenue without needing to acquire a single new customer.

Risks

The biggest risk, in my opinion, would be the OpenAI concentration, as the company is basically spending the money that Microsoft gave them. If the company fails to monetize its models or decides to pivot away from Azure, the massive backlog would evaporate. If OpenAI demands more independence or better terms, MSFT could lose its exclusive tollbooth status.

The U.S. FTC is also currently investigating Microsoft's cloud licensing practices and its partnership with OpenAI, looking to see if it is actually an un-notified merger. This makes Microsoft a primary target for regulators, as MSFT is now a gigantic tech company. The European Commission is also breathing down the company’s neck regarding the bundling of Teams with Office 365. A forced unbundling could slow down the adoption of new AI features.

Conclusion

I believe that the drawdown has been one of the best opportunities to invest in Microsoft in recent years, as the prospects are still great based on the unbelievable growth trajectory that is now trading at a 10% discount. The fiscal Q2 earnings were great, and I upgraded my rating on Microsoft to a Strong Buy.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Fraud Claim Dismissal Today Is Not A Sign Talc Litigation Is Over
data.description:
Investment Overview

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), the world's largest pharmaceutical by revenues, if not by market cap valuation - that honor falls to Eli Lilly (LLY) - has seen its shares gain by >35% over the past six months, compared to a 48% 12-month gain, and 38% 5-year gain (at the time of writing).

Current market cap is $544bn, and share price is $226 - a highest-ever valuation for a company whose shares traded at ~$142 at the beginning of January 2025.

JNJ released a solid set of Q4 and full-year earnings last week - revenues of $94.2bn, split between pharmaceuticals sales of $60.4bn, and MedTech sales of $33.8bn, up overall by 6% year-on-year on a constant currency basis, and diluted earnings per share of $11.03, which indicates price to sales ("P/S") and price to earnings ("P/E") of ~5.9x, and ~20.5 respectively.

Guidance for 2026, for adjusted operational sales growth of 5.4% - 6.4%, to ~$100bn, and adjusted EPS of $11.28 - $11.48 also seems to have cheered the market - stock rose from ~$215 per share, to ~$230 a few days after the announcement - and investors have been greeted with news of a win in the courts today.

News has broken that a New Jersey federal judge has dismissed a lawsuit brought against the Pharma - according to Seeking Alpha:

U.S. District Judge Michael Shipp dismissed the proposed class action lawsuit filed in the New Jersey federal court in May 2024 by five cancer victims suing the pharma giant over its talc-based products.

The plaintiffs alleged that the company’s bankruptcy strategy put billions of dollars out of the plaintiffs' reach in order to "hinder, delay, and defraud these women and prevent them from ever having their day in court."

Judge Shipp ruled on Thursday that the cancer victims didn’t have standing to sue as they couldn’t assert that they were harmed by J&J’s (JNJ) bankruptcy maneuver, which prevented their cases from advancing in courts between October 2021 and March 2025.

Context To Today's Ruling - Talc Litigation Continues To Weigh On Pharma

For some years, any investment in Johnson & Johnson has carried the caveat that the company is in the midst of a complex legal case which could see it pay out many billions of dollars, or perhaps even a double-digit billion sum, to claimants. As I wrote when covering JNJ's Q2 earnings in July last year:

In Q1 2025, JNJ had recorded a litigation-related expense of $(6.91bn), reflecting its attempts to settle "thousands" of lawsuits brought against the company, alleging that its discontinued baby powder and other talc-related products caused ovarian cancer.

JNJ has attempted three times to spin out the lawsuits into a separate company and declare that company bankrupt, which would cap its overall payout at the ~$10bn mark, but bankruptcy judges have refused the Pharma permission to do so, and JNJ, as I noted in my coverage of its Q1 earnings, says it "will return to the tort system to litigate and defeat these meritless talc claims."

In its Q3 2025 quarterly report / 100-Q filing (the 2025 annual report is not yet out at the time of writing, so far as I am aware), JNJ acknowledges that:

A significant number of personal injury claims alleging that talc causes cancer have been asserted against the Company and its affiliates arising out of the use of body powders containing talc, primarily JOHNSON’S Baby Powder.

The 10-Q discusses JNJ's various attempts to settle the vast majority of claims through its subsidiary company's voluntary petition for bankruptcy, but goes on to state as follows:

On March 31, 2025, the Texas Bankruptcy Court issued an order dismissing the case (the Texas dismissal) and, as a result, the Company reversed substantially all, or approximately $7 billion, from amounts previously reserved for the bankruptcy resolution. As of the third quarter 2025, the total present value of the reserve is approximately $3.8 billion, comprising previously executed settlement agreements, litigation defense and other costs. Approximately one-third of the reserve is recorded as a current liability.

After the Texas dismissal, the Company announced it would not appeal the decision and returned to the tort system to litigate the talc claims and defend the safety of its products. Courts have been holding scheduling conferences and the Company is preparing to start bellwether trials in consolidated proceedings in the California JCCP in November 2025 and in the New Jersey MCL in January 2026. Additionally, ovarian cancer trials are being scheduled in various state courts throughout 2026 and beyond. In the MDL, the Court is addressing the Company's Daubert motions, which are expected to be decided by the first quarter of 2026.

Clearly, the lawsuit dismissed today, which was brought by just five claimants, is a drop in the ocean when we consider that ~67k lawsuits have been brought against JNJ, and the company has a mixed record when it comes to defending itself.

JNJ has had many cases dismissed or negotiated down after appeal - according to its 10-Q, for example, the company paid out $2.5bn in 2021, after appeal, the original award having been $4.7bn. According to Reuters, a $1.5bn award was made last December in Baltimore to a single claimant who alleged decades of exposure to asbestos in the company’s talc-based products caused her peritoneal mesothelioma, although JNJ has said it intends to appeal the decision.

In December last year, JNJ paid out $40m to two women who had alleged JNJ's baby powder was responsible for their ovarian cancer, and a Los Angeles court awarded a claimant $966m in a ruling in October last year.

It is clearly an emotive subject - while JNJ has withdrawn talc-based baby powder from all markets globally, it continues to insist the product did not cause cancer, although a few days ago, a Special Master (court appointed neutral expert) on the case and former District Judge released a report recommending that women with ovarian cancer should be permitted to present medical and scientific experts to testify that JNJ's products were responsible for their illnesses. JNJ apparently plans to contest the ruling.

Clearly, it is practically impossible for JNJ to pay out 7 figure sums to every claimant - the company would have to declare itself bankrupt in such a scenario, let alone its spun-out companies. Equally clearly, the market is not factoring in the risk of JNJ paying out tens of billions of dollars to claimants in 2026, or even, perhaps, this decade, although JNJ is certainly a cash rich company.

According to its Q4 2025 earnings call transcript, management reported that:

We ended 2025 with approximately $20 billion of cash and marketable securities and $48 billion of debt for a net debt position of approximately $28 billion. The company generated $19.7 billion of free cash flow during 2025, on par with 2024, despite increased capital investment in the U.S. and the impact of tariffs.

Our financial strength is a competitive advantage that allows us to both invest in our future and return value to our shareholders. As we move forward in 2026, we expect to elevate free cash flow generation to approximately $21 billion.

Regarding the ongoing litigation, however, JNJ's Chief Financial Officer told analysts:

The bottom line is this is not going to change our strategy. We will continue to aggressively fight in the court system each and every one of these meritless claims. We will do so whether it's at original trial or through appeal. And we will continue to really bring to light the actions of the plaintiffs' bar, the tactics that they use, the third-party litigation financing, all which is really undermining U.S. business and U.S. competitiveness overall.

JNJ's CEO Joaquin Duato was equally as firm:

I would tell investors, we have been navigating this talc issue already for a decade. And we have been able to continue to deliver excellent results, invest in our business and continue to return value to shareholders. So let's focus on the real story here. The real story is our successful 2025, the strong guidance for 2026, and what you said before, our line of sight for double-digit growth in the later part of the decade.

Looking Ahead - Is JNJ A Buy In 2026 Based On Fair Assessment of Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities and Threats?

It is certainly not easy to poke holes in JNJ's business model - the company boasts assets such as cancer med Darzalex, >$14bn revenues in 2025, Erleada and Imbruvica, also oncology drugs contributing a combined >$6bn of revenues last year. >$6bn per annum Stelara's patent have expired, meaning its peak revenue years are now behind it, but Tremfya, another immunology med, more recently launched, is already a >$5bn per annum selling asset, and soon to be approved icontrokinra is a >$7bn per annum drug in waiting, analysts believe. Nipocalimab, an oral anti-inflammatory drug, is also expected to drive peak revenues in the ~$3bn per annum range.

JNJ's medical devices division grow by >6% revenues-wise in 2025, and there are plenty of pipeline catalysts to look out for in 2026 (shown below - source, JNJ Q4 earnings presentation), which if successful, further de-risk and future-proof the company.

In the table below, I have provided a rough guide to how I see JNJ's product sales changing between today and 2030, using historical revenue figures, and internal (management) and external (analysts) guidance, and as we can see, I think the business could approach the ~$80bn mark by 2030 (patent expiries are highlighted red).

At a CAGR of ~6%, the medical devices business may be worth $45bn per annum by 2030, making for a ~$120bn revenue business by the end of the decade, perhaps.

Of course there are headwinds to consider - the current drug pricing environment, for example, with the Trump administration cutting deals on a Pharma by Pharma basis while threatening "Most Favored Nation ("MFN") pricing and tariffs on pharma imports - and the talc litigation, which is certainly not going away, and is likely to intensify this year as more cases are heard, and with opposing sides moving no closer to any form of overall agreement.

That is, in fact, why, although I see the logic of investing in JNJ as a buy and hold over many years, and also benefitting from a dividend that yields ~2.3%, I rarely promote it to the top of my "must buy" pharma stock list.

In a pharmaceutical market currently obsessed with obesity and the GLP-1 class, hence why Eli Lilly - and its >$35bn per annum GLP-1 drugs Wegovy and Zepbound - is valued nearly twice as high as JNJ, JNJ is surprisingly not competing in this field, and although share price performance in 2025 was stellar, in the past decade or so that has been the exception, not the rule.

It is hard to forecast anything other than long-term growth for JNJ, for the top and bottom lines, but my feeling is the market is running slightly ahead in its estimate of JNJ's true intrinsic worth with its current valuation of the company. Hence, I'd award JNJ a Hold recommendation at the present time, while the stock trades at all-time high prices.

data.title: Meta's Story Just Flipped From CapEx Cycle To AI Profit Cycle
data.description:
In my last coverage on Meta Platforms (META), I was bullish coming out of the post-Q3 selloff and reiterated a strong buy heading into Q4 earnings, citing a shift in narrative as the main driver behind my rating.

This Q4 print largely supports my stance.

The quarter was another classic beat-and-raise, with Q1 2026 revenue guidance coming in well ahead of consensus (representing 30% yoy growth at the midpoint), shifting the narrative from data center overbuild to AI beneficiary.

In my view, the key metric that supports this shift is the mid single-digit increase in price per ad. On top of that, management highlighted that redistribution delivered a nearly 4× larger revenue impact than ad-load increases in H2 2025.

The CapEx guidance for the year came in significantly higher than my estimated $106–$119B range. As a side note, it was even higher than the Street's estimates as well. Yet, the stock didn't react like in Q3 earnings, when investors were spooked about this metric.

That said, I still see some debate around margins. Expenses grew faster than revenue in Q4 (40% vs. 24%), margins compressed, and FY26 expense guidance ($162–$169B) plus elevated CapEx guidance ($115–$135B) raises the probability that peak margins are behind us.

I still reiterate my strong buy rating. Even after the double-digit gain on Thursday, Meta is still cheaper on a forward P/E basis than the rest of the Mag 7. On top of that, I think the shift in narrative has the potential to drive shares back to all-time highs within the first half of the year.

Q4 Highlights

Q4 was a Meta classic beat and raise quarter.

That said, earnings beat consensus in Q4 by approximately as much as in Q3.

Q4 was far from a blowout quarter. In fact, the growth of the Family of Apps segment came in at a sequential 130 bps decline, falling to beat the growth in Q3.

This sequential miss was despite the 6% yoy increase in the global price per ad and the 18% yoy increase in impressions. Here is the CFO's rationale during the earnings call:

The average price per ad increased 6% year-over-year, benefiting from increased advertiser demand, largely driven by improved ad performance.

That improvement in ad performance was largely attributed to AI-driven improvements to redistribute ads across users rather than an increase in ad load:

In fact, in the second half of 2025, our initiatives on Facebook to redistribute ads across users and sessions delivered a nearly 4x larger revenue impact than Facebook ad load increases.

Another interesting remark was the $10B combined run rate of video generation tools, with sequential growth outpacing overall ads revenue growth by nearly 3×. I admit I was quite impressed by this figure, especially given the intense competition from OpenAI’s Sora Grok and Alphabet’s Veo video generation models.

In my earnings preview, I was quite bullish on the recent momentum in messaging revenue. Well, one of the highlights in Q4 was the 54% yoy increase in the Family of Apps other revenue segment, which was mainly driven by WhatsApp paid messaging growth and Meta Verified subscriptions.

Specifically, the company disclosed crossing a $2B annual run rate for WhatsApp paid messaging in Q4. While this is less than the $10B ARR from video-generation tools, I think Meta is only in the early stages of growth for these kinds of ads. US click-to-message ads revenue grew over 50% yoy in Q4, and I strongly believe that AI adoption among advertisers is the main driver behind this increase.

On the operating loss in the Reality Labs segment, I think the market felt some relief when management guided FY26 losses to stay similar to 2025. Revenue in this segment declined by double digits in Q4, although I’m not excessively worried, as Q4 last year had a tailwind from the Quest product-cycle release.

Finally, from a guidance perspective, Q1 2026 revenue was guided between $53.5B–$56.5B. That was significantly higher than the consensus of $51.4B, which led to higher sales revisions for the next 4 quarters.

That said, the consensus for EPS remained relatively flat after earnings. I discuss below why.

Risks: Margin Peak May Be Behind It

First, compare the chart below (EPS expectations for the next four quarters) with the chart above representing sales expectations.

Do you see the divergence after the Q3 and Q4 2025 earnings release? I believe this trend will continue this year. Why?

The growth in expenses is now higher than the revenue growth. Specifically, Q4 expenses were up 40% yoy vs 24% sales growth. Looking at operating margins, Q4 came in at 41% vs 48% a year ago.

The decline since Q4 2024 is clearly seen in the chart above. One of the pillars that supports the margin peak fear is the FY26 guidance for total expenses. Management is guiding a range between $162–$169B, driven by the following factors:

Looking at the second factor, despite the buzz around tech layoffs due to AI replacing white-collar jobs, the overall headcount of the company increased by 6% in 2025, totaling 78,865.

The other factor pressuring margins is CapEx, mainly through the depreciation costs of GPUs and neocloud leasing spending. Heading into the print, I estimated a FY26 CapEx range between $106-119B. The Street was looking for a midpoint CapEx of around $110.7B.

The actual CapEx guidance for this year came in at $115–$135B, significantly higher than estimates. However, this time, the market wasn't spooked by CapEx.

Why?

To be honest, it's difficult to reverse-engineer a price move like the one we've seen on Thursday. My theory is that it had less to do with Q4 results and more to do with the overreaction after Q3 earnings. Bear in mind, fundamentals in the Q3 release were also solid. The only factor that drove the selloff in Q3 was the fear that CapEx would be significantly higher in '26 than in '25. Well, I guess it’s fair to say that this fear was priced in heading into Q4, and the market focused instead on the AI tailwind in ad growth.

Valuation

In one word, cheap. On a one-year forward P/E basis, Meta is the cheapest stock among the Mag 7.

I think the stock has potential to re-rate further now that the CapEx cycle fear is mostly priced in. For reference, if Meta were to reach all-time highs, it would trade at 32x next year's earnings. That would still screen as one of the cheapest in the Mag 7 group.

Conclusion

I reiterate my strong buy on Meta.

I think the narrative has shifted from data center overbuild to AI beneficiary. A clear proof is the increase in ad pricing by 6% yoy in Q4, with redistribution initiatives accounting for a nearly 4× larger revenue impact than ad-load increases in H2 2025. At the very least, I'm looking for mid-single-digit growth in ad pricing in each quarter this year. An acceleration to double-digit growth would be the clearest signal that the ad recommendation algorithm is truly benefiting from AI.

On the risk side, I would closely monitor where operating margins land this year. Q4 already showed a margin compression, and the FY26 expenses guidance of $162–$169B could mean that the margin peak is behind. Overall, I think the market will look past this headwind, as long as the ads business keeps delivering mid- to high-20% growth. As a side note, the midpoint of the revenue guidance for Q1 2026 implies a 30% YoY growth rate.

From a price action perspective, the chart below looks quite attractive, and I think we could see new highs within the first half of this year.

data.title: Before Betting On Bubble Burst, Let's Check Its Existence First
data.description:
How does Ray Dalio diagnose market bubbles?

As all major market indices (such as the DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and also small cap RTY) kept pushing new highs, I’ve read more fair share of bubbe warnings lately. Inevitably, some of these warnings also come with some actionable strategies to bet on the popping of the bubble. Intrigued by these warnings, I’ve decided to run my own diagnosis and see how bad the bubble is.

I speak of my own diagnosis, but the fundamental ideas all come from Ray Dalio. In his book entitled Principles for Navigating Big Debt Crises, Dalio developed a framework to diagnose market bubbles. The framework uses 7 simple signs (all quoted below), which have correlated well with past data, as shown in the next table. Given the demonstrated effectiveness of this frame, I will next check the current market against this list item by item. For those who cannot wait for the bottom line, in the end, the diagnosis has not only left me unconvinced of the bubble warnings but also led me to question their existence.

1. High Prices Relative to Traditional Measures: This means stock prices are significantly higher than what metrics like the P/E ratio suggest they should be.

2. Influx of New and Inexperienced Investors: When a lot of people new to investing jump in due to a hot market, it can be a sign of frothy conditions.

3. Widespread Bullish Sentiment: Everyone is overwhelmingly optimistic about the market, with little concern for potential downsides.

4. Unsustainable Growth Expectations: The market is anticipating unrealistically high future growth for companies, which can't be maintained indefinitely.

5. Debt-Fueled Buying: Investors are borrowing heavily to buy stocks, which can amplify a bubble and make a crash more severe.

6. Speculative Purchases: People are buying stocks not based on company fundamentals but because they expect prices to keep rising regardless.

7. Does tightening risk popping the bubble?

Valuation, growth, and speculative purchases (signs 1, 4, and 6)

It is true that pretty much all indices are now trading at elevated valuation multiples in the current market (especially the Magnificent Seven). However, as Dalio’s framework emphasizes, valuation should be contextualized by other factors such as growth potential, profitability metrics, monetary/fiscal policies, etc.

In this section, let me focus on growth and profitability, and I will get to other aspects later. After examining the forward P/E ratios of various market indices, my assessment is that their valuation risks are well balanced by their growth potential and profitability. For instance, the chart next shows the forward P/E ratio of a few market segments, including the S&P 500 large, mid, and small caps. Starting with the large cap (aka the SP500), the FWD P/E ratio is about 21.8x vs. a TTM P/E of 31.2x as of this writing. As such, the implied EPS growth rate is more than 40%, resulting in a PEG ratio well below 1x either based on the TTM or the FWD P/E ratio. For the mid-cap and small-cap segments, the PEG ratios seem reasonable to me too. Plus, their current FWD P/E ratios (both around 16x) are well-aligned with the long-term historical baseline to start with.

This leaves us with the Magnificent 7, which are trading at an FWD P/E of 27.8, a significant premium above the overall market. Besides the P/E premium, these seven stocks also fuel the bubble warnings due to their heavy weights in various market indices. However, my view towards the SP500’s concentration in these mega-cap stocks is almost orthogonal to the views expressed in the bubble warnings. After adjusting for profitability, growth, and global market reach, these mega-caps actually carry lower risks in my view compared to the rest of the market. I will refrain from further elaboration here, as my thoughts on these stocks are provided in an earlier article entitled "2 arguments against S&P 500’s concentration risks."

Given the above, I certainly do not think today’s market rally is fueled by speculative purchases. This is a key difference, in my view, between the current market and the 2000s dot-com bubble, when many stocks with poor profits (or even no profits at all) traded at high prices. Whereas in today’s market, the stocks that make up the majority of the market indices (such as the MAG 7) enjoy superior profits and thus should naturally deserve a P/E premium.

Bullish sentiment and leverage (sign 3 and 5)

Moving on to signs 3 and 5, bullish sentiment and debt-fueled buying. For market sentiment, the indicator I watch the most is the following NAAIM Exposure Index:

It's published by the National Association of Active Investment Managers (“NAAIM”). NAAIM member firms reported equity exposure, with 0% indication a 100% cash or hedged to market neutral and 100% indicating 100% fully invested in equity.

I like the index because it reflects the sentiment of the active investment managers in the association and expect it to provide a unique perspective from professional active managers. To wit, the screenshot below shows the NAAIM’s index since mid-2025 to the present on a weekly basis. As seen, the current reading (as of Jan 28) hovers around 92.58. It is certainly towards the bullish end, but I see no warning signs of systematic use of leverage (again, a reading of 100 mean fully invested “only” and a reading of above 100 means use of leverage). Expanding the timeframe a bit, the index has fluctuated in a range between about 80 and 100 in the past 3 months or so, reflecting a quite measured risk appetite in my mind.

Other risks and final thoughts

Moving on to the rest of the signs. The 2nd checkpoint calls for an examination of the influx of new and inexperienced investors. I am not aware whether there is a way or index to single out fund flows due to only new and inexperienced investors. So, I will confess my ignorance on this sign and leave it in the unsure bin. In case you have insights on this front, please leave a comment below so I can follow up and learn.

Finally, the last checkpoint (#7) calls for an examination of tightening risks. I see very little risk on this front too. As you probably already know, the Federal Reserve decided to keep interest rates steady in the current range in its Wednesday meeting. This decision is in line with market expectations and represents a pause in rate cuts after lowering the Fed Fund Rates three cuts times in 2025. Looking ahead, the FOMC’s dot plot (see the chart below) points to continued cuts in the years ahead. President Donald Trump has been a very vocal supporter of further cuts too, and the candidates for the next Fed chair position are also prone to continue rate cuts in my view.

To wrap up, the goal of this article is to run a comprehensive check on the bubble signs from today’s market. Against the 7 symptoms in Ray Dalio’s framework, I either see no data exhibiting these symptoms (at least not to any alarming level, such as in the case of elevated P/E ratios) or remain skeptical/unsure about them. As such, I urge investors to rethink any strategy that calls for a bet on the burst of the market bubble. If you’ve unfortunately followed these strategies in recent years, you know how much your investment results have suffered from them. I see a high probability for poor results again in 2026 from these strategies.

data.title: Douglas Elliman: I Am Downgrading Shares Even Though The Industry Will Recover
data.description:
One of the more intriguing prospects in the market for investors to be aware of is Douglas Elliman (DOUG). The company has historically operated as a player in the housing market. Specifically, it serves as the owner of one of the largest residential brokerage companies in the New York metropolitan area. Through these activities, the company provides real estate services, namely the facilitation of the sale of properties. However, it does also have its own property technology solutions business, which management refers to as PropTech. Through this part of the company, management provides certain software-oriented technologies that offer users a plug-and-play environment that makes it easier for agents to engage in their real estate activities, with examples being a digital home search platform that the company has and an AI assistant app that helps to speed up certain tasks like generating market reports and writing property descriptions.

This is most certainly an interesting prospect from a conceptual standpoint. But unfortunately, over the last few years now, the business has come under pressure. We have seen a general decline in the number of transactions. Overall gross transaction value is still recovering from where it was back in 2022. But the good news is that, ever since bottoming out in 2023, revenue for the company has risen, with lower transactions offset by a rise in average transaction value and a partial recovery in gross transaction value. At some point, the housing market will recover nicely. And when it does, we should see some nice growth here. Having said that, one thing that I don't like about the institution is its overall financial performance. Despite revenue climbing again, profits and cash flows remain problematic. Given this, I don't think I can be bullish on the business any longer. In fact, I believe that rating it a "Sell" is the most logical choice. This would represent a major revision compared to the “Buy” I rated it back in August 2023.

Not a home for my money

As much as I wish I could be bullish about Douglas Elliman, I can't bring myself to do it. I don't believe that financial performance from the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years is terribly relevant considering that we are now in early 2026. But for context, in the chart above, you can see financial results for that window of time. We saw a decline in revenue from 2022 to 2023, followed by a partial recovery in 2024. Net profits, unfortunately, have actually worsened during that time. And cash flows, which were admittedly mixed, showed deterioration as well.

More important would be the results that we have seen as of late. And the most recent figures here cover the first nine months of 2025. The chart above shows exactly what I mean. During that time, revenue for the company was $787.6 million. That's up nicely from the $752.3 million the company reported a year earlier.

According to management, this growth in revenue came even though the number of transactions facilitated by the company dropped from 16,444 to 16,099. The chart above illustrates the overall trend that we have seen in transactions in recent years. After posting just a small recovery in 2024, there was a continuation of decline heading into 2025.

The National Association of Realtors provides relatively detailed data when it comes to existing home sales. During 2025, they said that in the United States, we saw 4.06 million existing homes sold. That matches what was seen in 2024 but was down slightly from the 4.09 million reported for 2023. While existing home sales across the United States have been essentially flat, new home sales have been depressed in recent years. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. Because interest rates are dropping, we have seen a jump as of late. But we are still below where we were a couple of years ago by a pretty substantial margin. What this does show is that the overall housing market has been weak but uneven. In previous articles, I detailed some of the challenges that the industry is facing. In particular, years of inflationary pressures that pushed prices up, and now interest rates aimed at combating those same pressures, have made housing unaffordable to many Americans.

As the number of transactions that the company facilitated declined, it did see some offset from this by an increase in gross transaction value that was thanks to a rise in the average transaction value. The average home sold during the first nine months of 2025 was worth $1.87 million. That was up quite a bit from the $1.68 million reported a year earlier. Back in 2022, the average transaction value was only $1.62 million. That's still a massive amount of money, but it is illustrative of the fact that price inflation has played a role in the company's financial results.

Unfortunately, even though revenue has risen of late, financial performance on the bottom line remains under pressure. The company generated a net loss of $53.3 million for the first nine months of 2025. That is better than the $70.3 million reported a year earlier. Cash flows have also gotten better, but they are paltry in the grand scheme of things. The company achieved operating cash flow of only $0.5 million during this window of time. That compares to the $17 million outflow that it experienced a year earlier. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we do get an improvement from negative $13.3 million to positive $13.2 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business shifted from negative $12.4 million to positive $2.9 million.

Moving forward, management has some pretty ambitious objectives. For 2026, for instance, the firm is leveraging its position as a player dedicated largely to the pure-play luxury space to turn its focus toward international expansion. This includes growth opportunities in France and Monaco. Estimates are difficult to come by when it comes to these markets. But according to one source, published earlier this year, the French property market started turning around in a big way in 2025. In fact, unit sales were up 11% year over year. That same source did not really provide any concrete data when it comes to the luxury space specifically. But it did describe the luxury market as “resilient.” And in Monaco, the housing market appears to be alive and well. In fact, one source said that in 2024 the average price of primary units in that area reached $39.4 million. So this is definitely a market for a firm such as Douglas Elliman to participate in.

Valuing a company such as this is a bit tricky. After all, I typically value companies based on profits and cash flows. But we really don't have that luxury here. What I will say is that, while much cheaper on an enterprise value-to-revenue basis than it was a few years ago, the current trading multiple, shown in the chart above, is most certainly near the higher end of the range that we have seen since markets eased following the end of the COVID-19 pandemic. More telling is the price-to-book multiple. The chart below illustrates that the company is trading well above any point that it has since at least early 2022. This also indicates optimism from market participants that the future will look brighter. But it also signals potential overvaluation.

Takeaway

Conceptually, I find Douglas Elliman to be an interesting prospect. I definitely appreciate that management is focused on growth opportunities. But when you look at the fundamentals, it's difficult to get anything other than a bit bearish. Yes, I know that times have been tough. But you would think that at a time when revenue is growing, the company would at least be able to achieve consistent positive cash flow. Add on top of this how the stock is valued compared to where it has been valued previously, and I think that assigning it a soft “Sell” rating makes sense.
